summarization.a: TMA pulse at 100°C: Correlates surface deposition rate of O⟨s⟩ (red), TMA mole fraction (black), and *Al(CH₃)₂ surface coverage (blue). Deposition rate peaks around 5 ms then drops as surface coverage saturates to 50%, despite continued TMA concentration increase.
summarization.b: TMA pulse at 150°C: Faster surface saturation than 100°C, with peak deposition occurring earlier. The dotted line at t=0.004s shows temperature comparison across panels.
summarization.c: TMA pulse at 200°C: Surface saturation occurs even earlier. Peak deposition rate remains similar but is reached more quickly.
summarization.d: TMA pulse at 250°C: Fastest saturation; surface coverage reaches 50% before 0.004s. At this point, deposition rate at 250°C is slightly lower than 200°C because surface is already saturated.

a.question: What happens to the deposition rate once *Al(CH₃)₂ surface coverage reaches 50%?
a.answer: The deposition rate drops drastically once surface coverage saturates at 50% (the other half is occupied by *AlCH₃). Even though TMA concentration continues to increase, no further deposition occurs because all reactive *OH surface sites have been consumed. This demonstrates the self-limiting nature of ALD.

a.question: What do the three plotted variables represent?
a.answer: Red circles (left axis): mass deposition rate of bulk oxygen species O⟨s⟩; black squares (right axis): TMA gas-phase mole fraction; blue diamonds (right axis): *Al(CH₃)₂ surface species coverage.

a.question: Is the peak deposition rate dependent on process temperature?
a.answer: No. The peak deposition rate values are nearly identical (~1.6×10⁻⁴ kg/m²·s) across all four temperatures; only the time to reach the peak changes.

d.question: How does temperature affect the time to reach surface saturation?
d.answer: Higher temperature accelerates saturation. The dotted lines show saturation is reached at approximately t=0.004s for 100°C but progressively earlier at higher temperatures, with 250°C saturating fastest.
